pois.short: A Sancerre family's Marlborough outpost — organic wines tasted in a converted chapel among the vines.
pois.commentary: When the Bourgeois family of Sancerre fame planted vines in Marlborough, they brought 10 generations of Loire Valley winemaking with them. The cellar door is a beautifully converted chapel surrounded by organic vineyards. Their Sauvignon Blanc and Pinot Noir show a distinctly French restraint — mineral, textured, and age-worthy. By appointment only, which keeps it beautifully uncrowded.

reviews.summary: Wine lovers praise the unique French-meets-Marlborough style. The chapel setting is frequently described as 'magical.' The appointment-only policy can be a hurdle, but visitors consistently say it's worth planning ahead.

pois.name: Huia Vineyards
pois.short: Certified organic boutique estate crafting elegant Pinot Gris, Chardonnay and méthode traditionnelle sparkling.
pois.commentary: Claire and Mike Allan have been making certified organic wines since 1990, long before it was fashionable. Their méthode traditionnelle sparkling is one of the best in the country, and the Gewürztraminer is perfumed and off-dry in the Alsatian style. The small cellar door on Boyces Road feels like visiting friends — the Allans often pour the wines themselves.

reviews.summary: Organic wine fans love the commitment to sustainability. The sparkling wines are called 'seriously underrated.' Some visitors note the modest facilities, but the personal service and wine quality more than compensate.

pois.name: Nautilus Estate
pois.short: Family-owned estate with standout Pinot Noir and an intimate tasting room in Rapaura.
pois.commentary: Part of the S. Smith & Son family (Yalumba in Australia), Nautilus has quietly built a reputation for Pinot Noir that rivals Central Otago's best. The cellar door in Rapaura is small and personal — you'll often taste with the winemaking team. Their Cuvée Marlborough sparkling is excellent value and the Opawa range is a great introduction to the estate style.

reviews.summary: Wine lovers appreciate the quality-to-price ratio and personal service. The Pinot Noir consistently impresses. The cellar door is modest compared to flashier neighbours, but regulars consider that part of the charm.

pois.name: Te Whare Ra Wines
pois.short: One of Marlborough's oldest vineyards — planted 1979 — now producing benchmark aromatic whites.
pois.commentary: Te Whare Ra (TWR) was one of the first vineyards planted in Marlborough and today Jason and Anna Flowerday craft some of the region's most compelling aromatic wines. Their Gewürztraminer and Riesling regularly win gold medals, and the old-vine complexity is unmistakable. The cellar door is by appointment, ensuring an unhurried, personal experience with some of Marlborough's most thoughtful wines.

reviews.summary: Serious wine enthusiasts call TWR a hidden gem. The aromatic whites are praised as among the best in the country. By-appointment visits mean no crowds, though spontaneous visitors may miss out.

pois.name: Arbour Restaurant
pois.short: Blenheim's top fine-dining restaurant — seasonal Marlborough produce with an all-NZ wine list.
pois.commentary: Chef Bradley Hornby's Arbour has become a destination in its own right, drawing food lovers to Blenheim for multi-course tasting menus built around whatever's in season. The wine list is an encyclopaedia of Marlborough's best, with deep verticals from producers most visitors haven't heard of. The dining room is intimate — 30 covers — so booking well ahead is essential.

reviews.summary: Diners call Arbour the best restaurant in the top of the South Island. The tasting menus are praised for creativity and local sourcing. A few note the limited seating means it can book out weeks ahead, especially in summer.

pois.name: Wairau River Wines
pois.short: Family-owned vineyard with a popular restaurant set among the vines near Renwick.
pois.commentary: Phil and Chris Rose planted their first vines in 1978, making Wairau River one of Marlborough's pioneers. The on-site restaurant serves locally sourced dishes under old trees beside the vineyard — it's one of the most pleasant lunch spots in the region. The reserve Sauvignon Blanc shows what the variety can do with a bit of age, and the Pinot Noir is consistently strong.

reviews.summary: The restaurant draws praise for its setting among the vines and the quality of the food-wine pairing. Families appreciate the relaxed atmosphere. Some say the wine list could extend beyond the estate, but most are happy with the range.

pois.name: Saint Clair Family Estate
pois.short: Award-winning family estate in Blenheim with a vineyard kitchen and one of the region's best cellar doors.
pois.commentary: Saint Clair has amassed more awards than almost any other Marlborough winery, yet remains family-owned and unpretentious. The Vineyard Kitchen serves excellent woodfired pizzas and seasonal plates right beside the vines. Their Pioneer Block series showcases the diversity of Marlborough's sub-regions — try the Block 1 Foundation Sauvignon for a textbook example of the style.

reviews.summary: Visitors love the vineyard kitchen's relaxed vibe and woodfired pizzas. The wine range offers great quality at every price point. Some note it's busier than boutique alternatives, but the consistency and value keep people returning.

pois.name: Mahi Wines
pois.short: Boutique winemaker Brian Bicknell's passion project — single-vineyard wines from Marlborough's Twin Valleys.
pois.commentary: Brian Bicknell worked at Seresin and Framingham before launching Mahi, which means 'our work' in te reo Māori. The cellar door is in a character building near Renwick and the tasting experience is deeply personal — Brian often pours himself. His single-vineyard Sauvignon Blancs and Pinot Noirs reveal the subtle differences between Marlborough's sub-regions in a way few wineries manage.

reviews.summary: Wine geeks love the single-vineyard focus and Brian's encyclopedic knowledge of Marlborough terroir. The by-appointment model keeps it intimate. A few casual visitors wish for drop-in availability.

pois.name: Seresin Estate
pois.short: Biodynamic estate founded by filmmaker Michael Seresin — olive oil, wine and a beautiful cellar door.
pois.commentary: Film cinematographer Michael Seresin (Harry Potter, Midnight Express) established this biodynamic estate in the 1990s. Beyond excellent Pinot Noir and Chardonnay, Seresin produces some of New Zealand's finest olive oil from 4,000 trees. The cellar door is architecturally striking, set among lavender gardens. Their Memento Pinot Noir is a benchmark of the variety in Marlborough.

reviews.summary: Visitors love the biodynamic story and the quality of both wine and olive oil. The cellar door setting is frequently called beautiful. Some find the wines pricier than neighbours, but most agree the quality justifies it.

pois.name: Dog Point Vineyard
pois.short: Iconic Marlborough estate from former Cloudy Bay winemakers — Section 94 Sauvignon is a modern classic.
pois.commentary: Ivan Sutherland and James Healy left Cloudy Bay to start Dog Point, and their Section 94 Sauvignon Blanc — wild-fermented in old oak — challenged everything people thought they knew about the variety. The Chardonnay is equally compelling. The cellar door is open by appointment and visits feel exclusive and educational. This is serious wine for people who've moved beyond simple fruit-driven styles.

reviews.summary: Critics and visitors alike call Section 94 one of New Zealand's great white wines. The tasting experience is deeply informative. By-appointment only, so planning ahead is essential.

pois.name: Brancott Estate Heritage Centre
pois.short: Where Marlborough wine began — the heritage centre sits above the original 1973 Montana plantings.
pois.commentary: In 1973, Montana Wines (now Brancott Estate) planted the first commercial Sauvignon Blanc vines in Marlborough on land everyone said was too dry. That gamble created a $2 billion industry. The Heritage Centre sits on the hillside above those original vines with panoramic views across the Wairau Valley. The restaurant serves excellent bistro-style food, and the interactive wine-blending experience is great fun.

reviews.summary: History buffs appreciate the birthplace-of-Marlborough-wine story. The views from the restaurant terrace are stunning. Some find the corporate feel less charming than boutique alternatives, but the food quality and wine-blending activity win people over.

pois.name: Rock Ferry Wines
pois.short: Certified organic winery pushing into natural winemaking — the Trig Hill wines are beautifully textured.
pois.commentary: Rock Ferry has been organic since day one and is now exploring natural winemaking with their Trig Hill range — minimal intervention, wild ferments, skin-contact whites. The cellar door in Rapaura is unpretentious and the team is passionate about explaining their approach. If you're curious about the natural wine movement in New Zealand, this is a great place to start.

reviews.summary: Natural wine fans love the Trig Hill range. The organic commitment is genuine, not marketing. Some conventional palates find the natural wines challenging, but the standard range is crowd-pleasing.

pois.name: Marlborough Farmers' Market
pois.short: Sunday morning market at the A&P showgrounds — Marlborough's best local produce, baking and artisan foods.
pois.commentary: Every Sunday morning, the A&P Showgrounds in Blenheim fill with stalls selling Marlborough's bounty: green-lipped mussels, locally smoked salmon, artisan cheeses, organic vegetables, fresh bread and pastries. It's the best way to taste the region beyond the wineries. Get there by 9am for the best selection — the salmon and mussel stalls sell out fast. Great coffee too.

reviews.summary: Locals and visitors alike praise the quality and variety. The smoked salmon and mussel stalls are particular highlights. Early birds get the best picks — stallholders start packing up by noon.

pois.name: Scotch Wine Bar
pois.short: Blenheim's best wine bar — 30+ Marlborough wines by the glass with tapas-style sharing plates.
pois.commentary: Scotch fills a gap in Blenheim's evening scene with a curated selection of Marlborough wines by the glass, many from small producers that don't have cellar doors. The tapas-style food is a step above standard bar snacks — think local blue cod croquettes and Marlborough salmon tartare. It's the perfect place to end a day of winery visits with something a bit different.

reviews.summary: Visitors appreciate the wide by-the-glass selection and tapas menu. The staff's wine knowledge is praised. Some note Blenheim isn't a late-night town, so don't expect big-city energy.

pois.name: Gibbs on Godfrey
pois.short: Popular Blenheim café and restaurant — excellent brunch, seasonal menus and great local wine list.
pois.commentary: Gibbs has become Blenheim's go-to for a proper brunch or relaxed dinner. The menu changes with the seasons, sourcing heavily from Marlborough producers — expect Cloudy Bay clams, local lamb and seasonal greens. The wine list leans into small-batch Marlborough producers, and the staff are happy to guide you through it. The courtyard garden is lovely on a summer evening.

reviews.summary: Brunch gets consistent praise, with the eggs Benedict and seasonal specials as highlights. The courtyard is popular in summer. Some find dinner service slow on busy nights.

pois.name: Golden Mile Brewing
pois.short: Blenheim's craft brewery — small-batch beers inspired by the wine region's terroir.
pois.commentary: In a region so dominated by wine, Golden Mile offers a refreshing alternative. Their taproom in central Blenheim pours a rotating selection of small-batch beers, many incorporating local ingredients — Marlborough hops, local honey, even Sauvignon Blanc must. It's a fun counterpoint to a day of wine tasting and the brewers are happy to chat about their process.

reviews.summary: Beer lovers appreciate having a craft option in wine country. The rotating taps keep things interesting. Some note the taproom is small and can feel cramped on busy weekends.

pois.name: Allan Scott Family Winemakers
pois.short: Marlborough pioneer winery with a popular vineyard restaurant — the Twelve Trees lunch is a classic.
pois.commentary: Allan Scott planted his first Marlborough vines in the early 1970s, making him one of the region's true pioneers. The Twelve Trees restaurant, shaded by its namesake trees, serves excellent seasonal food paired with estate wines. The Sauvignon Blanc and Riesling are reliable, and the Cecilia bubbles are excellent value. A Marlborough institution that's been welcoming visitors for decades.

reviews.summary: The Twelve Trees restaurant is a lunchtime favourite. The pioneer story adds depth. Some find the wines reliable rather than exciting, but the restaurant compensates.

pois.name: Villa Maria Marlborough
pois.short: New Zealand's most awarded winery — Marlborough cellar door showcasing the full premium range.
pois.commentary: Villa Maria is New Zealand's most awarded winery (and its largest family-owned one), and the Marlborough cellar door is the best place to taste their top-tier wines. The Reserve and Single Vineyard Sauvignon Blancs show impressive depth and complexity, and the Pinot Noir programme has improved markedly. The cellar door experience is polished and professional — good for both beginners and enthusiasts.

reviews.summary: The premium wines are excellent. The cellar door is professional and welcoming. Some find it corporate compared to smaller operations.

pois.name: Yealands Estate
pois.short: Sustainably farmed coastal estate in Seddon — cellar door with sea views and babydoll sheep among the vines.
pois.commentary: Yealands' Seaview vineyard on the east coast of Marlborough is one of the country's most sustainably farmed wine estates. Babydoll sheep graze between the vine rows (natural lawn mowers), and the cellar door offers views across the Cook Strait. The Peter Yealands range is excellent value, and the Reserve Sauvignon Blanc is impressive. The drive down from Blenheim is scenic along the coast.

reviews.summary: The sustainability story and sea-view setting impress. The babydoll sheep are a hit with visitors. Some note it's further from Blenheim than most wineries.

pois.name: Gramado's Restaurant
pois.short: Blenheim's popular steakhouse — NZ beef and lamb chargrilled with Marlborough wines.
pois.commentary: Gramado's is Blenheim's answer to the steak craving that inevitably hits after a day of wine tasting. The NZ beef and lamb are chargrilled to order, and the Marlborough wine list is deep and well-priced. The atmosphere is warm and lively, and the portions are generous. It's where locals go for a proper dinner — reliable, unpretentious and consistently good.

reviews.summary: Reliable steakhouse with good Marlborough wines. The grilled meats are consistently well-prepared. Some find the menu limited beyond steaks.

pois.name: Spy Valley Wines
pois.short: Architecturally striking cellar door near the former spy base — award-winning Sauvignon Blanc and Pinot Noir.
pois.commentary: Named after the nearby Waihopai spy base (visible from the vineyard), Spy Valley's modern cellar door is one of Marlborough's most visually striking. The wines are excellent across the range — the Envoy Sauvignon Blanc and Pinot Noir are consistently gold-medal winners. The tasting experience is well-structured and the staff are knowledgeable. A must-visit for architecture and wine lovers alike.

reviews.summary: The architecture and spy-base story add intrigue. The wines are consistently high quality. Some visitors are disappointed they can't actually see the spy base.

pois.name: Rock Ferry Cellar Door Kitchen
pois.short: Organic vineyard kitchen in Rapaura — seasonal plates with estate wines and garden views.
pois.commentary: Rock Ferry's cellar door kitchen serves seasonal plates using local organic produce to complement their wines. The garden setting among the Rapaura vineyards is lovely, and the food is simple but well-executed — think local cheese boards, seasonal salads and house-baked bread. It's a great casual lunch option between wine tastings, and the organic ethos carries through from vineyard to plate.

reviews.summary: Good casual vineyard dining. The organic commitment extends to the food. Some find the menu limited, but what's there is well-done.

pois.name: Giesen Wines Marlborough
pois.short: Pioneer of NZ's zero-alcohol wine movement — Marlborough cellar door with both full and dealcoholised ranges.
pois.commentary: The Giesen brothers from Germany planted their first Marlborough vines in 1981 and have since become pioneers of New Zealand's zero-alcohol wine category. The cellar door near Renwick offers tastings of both the traditional range and the award-winning Giesen 0% wines — a great option for designated drivers and non-drinkers on wine trails. The Sauvignon Blanc (both versions) is consistently good.

reviews.summary: The zero-alcohol wines surprise sceptics. A welcome inclusion for diverse groups. The traditional wines are solid Marlborough offerings.

pois.name: Queen Charlotte Sound — Dolphin Watch
pois.short: Bottlenose and dusky dolphins in the sheltered waters of Queen Charlotte Sound — cruise encounters.
pois.commentary: Queen Charlotte Sound's calm, deep waters are home to resident bottlenose dolphins and visiting dusky dolphins. Several operators run nature cruises from Picton that combine dolphin watching with scenic exploration of the sound's bays and islands. King shag and little blue penguin also seen.

reviews.summary: Beautiful cruise with good dolphin sighting rates. The sound itself is spectacular. Combine with a mail boat run.

pois.name: Long Island Marine Reserve
pois.short: Marlborough Sounds marine reserve — blue cod, crayfish, and kelp forests in crystal-clear water.
pois.commentary: Long Island Marine Reserve in Queen Charlotte Sound protects 619 hectares of marine habitat. Blue cod grow to impressive sizes, crayfish are abundant, and kelp forests shelter diverse reef communities. Accessible by water taxi from Picton. Snorkelling and diving.

reviews.summary: Impressive marine life. Water taxi access adds to the adventure. Best in calm conditions.

pois.name: Duffers Reef — King Shag Colony
pois.short: One of only four breeding colonies of the critically rare king shag — found only in the Marlborough Sounds.
pois.commentary: The king shag (kawau) is one of the world's rarest cormorants, with only about 600 individuals, all in the Marlborough Sounds. Duffers Reef hosts one of the four breeding colonies. Boat tours from Picton pass at a respectful distance for viewing.

reviews.summary: Rare bird sighting opportunity. Boat tours provide good viewing distance. Binoculars essential.

pois.name: Motuara Island Bird Sanctuary
pois.short: Predator-free island sanctuary — saddleback, South Island robin, and king shag in Queen Charlotte Sound.
pois.commentary: Motuara Island was cleared of predators and now hosts translocated South Island saddleback, South Island robin, and yellow-crowned kakariki. The island is also a king shag roosting site. Water taxi access from Picton with walking tracks to the summit viewpoint.

reviews.summary: Outstanding birding. The saddleback and robins are very confiding. Water taxi trip adds to the experience.

pois.name: Cape Campbell — Fur Seal Colony
pois.short: NZ fur seals haul out along the wild Marlborough coast near Cape Campbell — stunning coastal scenery.
pois.commentary: The rocky coastline around Cape Campbell hosts a growing NZ fur seal colony. Accessible via a coastal walk from the Awatere Valley road end. The seals are best viewed from a safe distance on the rock platforms. Spectacular views of the Kaikoura Ranges across the strait.

reviews.summary: Wild and scenic. The seals are impressive but keep your distance. Remote access adds to the experience.

pois.name: Wairau Lagoons Wetland
pois.short: Extensive wetland system at the mouth of the Wairau River — banded rail, bittern, and royal spoonbill.
pois.commentary: The Wairau Lagoons are a significant wetland at the mouth of the Wairau River near Blenheim. The diverse habitats support banded rail, Australasian bittern, royal spoonbill, and marsh crake. Walking and cycling tracks provide good viewing access.

reviews.summary: Excellent birding close to Blenheim. The cycle trail is a good way to explore. Best early morning.

pois.name: White's Bay — Little Blue Penguin Nesting
pois.short: Little blue penguins nest in the rock crevices around White's Bay — dusk viewing in a beautiful setting.
pois.commentary: White's Bay, a short drive from Blenheim, hosts a small colony of little blue penguins that nest in rock crevices and under boulders along the shore. Dusk viewing during breeding season can be rewarding. The bay itself is a popular picnic and swimming spot.

reviews.summary: Lovely bay with bonus penguin viewing. Timing is everything — arrive at dusk. Quiet and uncrowded.

pois.name: Pelorus Bridge — Giant Longfin Eels
pois.short: Crystal-clear river pools with giant longfin eels and native fish — walk through beautiful beech forest.
pois.commentary: Pelorus Bridge Scenic Reserve, famous as a Hobbit filming location, has crystal-clear swimming holes where you can see giant longfin eels in the pools. The surrounding beech forest holds bellbird, tomtit, and kereru. Short and long loop walks available.

reviews.summary: Beautiful spot. The eels are fascinating — some are enormous. The forest walk is excellent. Great for families.

pois.name: Para Wetland — Swamp Restoration
pois.short: Community-led wetland restoration supporting fernbird, spotless crake, and marsh crake.
pois.commentary: Para Wetland near Blenheim is a community-driven restoration project re-establishing native wetland habitat. Fernbird, spotless crake, and marsh crake have been recorded. The site demonstrates how even small wetland remnants can support significant biodiversity.

reviews.summary: Nice community conservation project. Small but productive for birding. Best visited with local knowledge.

pois.name: Maud Island Scientific Reserve
pois.short: Predator-free island reserve housing some of NZ's rarest species — Hamilton's frog, kakapo recovery base.
pois.commentary: Maud Island in Pelorus Sound is one of DOC's most important scientific reserves. Predator-free, it has been a key site for kakapo recovery and houses populations of Hamilton's frog and Maud Island frog — two of the world's most primitive frog species. Limited guided visits available.

reviews.summary: Incredible conservation site. Very restricted access but DOC occasionally runs guided visits. Unique wildlife.

pois.name: Vernon Lagoons — Wading Bird Haven
pois.short: Coastal lagoon system supporting diverse wading birds — pied stilt, godwit, and wrybill.
pois.commentary: The Vernon Lagoons near Blenheim are shallow coastal lagoons that attract diverse wading birds including pied stilt, bar-tailed godwit, wrybill, and banded dotterel. Access is easy along the lagoon margins. An underrated birding site.

reviews.summary: Underrated birding spot. Easy access from Blenheim. Low tide is best for wader viewing.

pois.name: Omaka Aviation Heritage Centre
pois.short: World-class aviation museum with WWI & WWII aircraft in cinematic displays designed by Peter Jackson's Weta Workshop.
pois.commentary: The Omaka Aviation Heritage Centre near Blenheim is one of the world's finest aviation museums, showcasing rare WWI and WWII aircraft in astonishingly realistic diorama settings created by Peter Jackson's Weta Workshop. The 'Knights of the Sky' exhibition features original and replica WWI aircraft in life-sized scenes depicting dramatic dogfights and crash landings, complete with mannequins, pyrotechnics, and landscaping of extraordinary detail. The 'Dangerous Skies' WWII exhibition continues the immersive approach. Many aircraft are from Sir Peter Jackson's personal collection, including extremely rare original machines. The level of theatrical presentation sets Omaka apart from any other aviation museum globally. Even visitors with no particular interest in aviation find themselves captivated. Allow at least two hours. The biennial Classic Fighters airshow (Easter, even years) sees vintage aircraft take to the skies. Located at Omaka Aerodrome, a short drive from central Blenheim.

reviews.summary: Absolutely world-class — the Weta Workshop dioramas are jaw-dropping in their detail and drama. You don't need to be an aviation enthusiast to be blown away. One of the best museums in New Zealand.

pois.name: Millennium Art Gallery Blenheim
pois.short: Blenheim's public art gallery showcasing Marlborough artists alongside touring national exhibitions.
pois.commentary: The Millennium Art Gallery is Blenheim's public gallery space, housed in a purpose-built facility that presents a regular programme of exhibitions featuring Marlborough and wider New Zealand artists. The gallery rotates exhibitions every few weeks, showcasing painting, sculpture, ceramics, photography, and mixed media from both established and emerging artists. Regular touring exhibitions from national institutions add broader context. The gallery is run by the Marlborough Arts Council and serves as a community hub for the region's active arts scene. Admission is free. The gallery sits in Blenheim's Seymour Square precinct, a pleasant garden setting in the centre of town near the public library and clock tower. Allow about an hour, less if between major exhibitions. The gallery shop offers locally made art and craft. A nice complement to a Marlborough wine-touring day, providing cultural balance to the region's famous viticultural offerings.

reviews.summary: A pleasant community gallery with rotating exhibitions of local art. Nothing blockbuster but always something interesting on display. Free entry makes it a nice stop while in Blenheim.

pois.name: Picton Foreshore Heritage Trail
pois.short: Scenic waterfront heritage walk through Picton's maritime history with interpretive panels and harbour views.
pois.commentary: The Picton Foreshore Heritage Trail is a scenic walking route along the waterfront of this historic port town at the head of Queen Charlotte Sound. Interpretive panels along the route tell the story of Picton's rich maritime history, from early Māori settlement and European whaling to the development of the inter-island ferry service that makes Picton the gateway between New Zealand's North and South Islands. The trail passes the Edwin Fox Maritime Museum (housing the world's ninth oldest surviving ship), the whaling exhibits, historic boat sheds, and the busy ferry terminal. The walk takes in beautiful harbour views with the green hills of the Marlborough Sounds as backdrop. Picton's compact town centre offers excellent cafés and seafood restaurants. The trail is flat and accessible, ideal for filling time before or after a ferry crossing. Allow about 1-1.5 hours for the full trail, less if combined with a café stop.

reviews.summary: A pleasant way to explore Picton with good historical information along the way. The harbour views are lovely and the Edwin Fox is fascinating. Perfect for killing time before a ferry.

pois.name: Robin Hood Bay Beach
pois.short: Secluded Marlborough Sounds beach with clear water, native bush setting, and peaceful swimming.
pois.commentary: Robin Hood Bay is a small, secluded beach on the eastern side of the Marlborough Sounds, accessible by road from Picton via a scenic drive through Port Underwood Road. The beach offers sheltered swimming in clear, calm water, backed by native bush and farmland. Unlike the more famous Queen Charlotte Sound beaches, Robin Hood Bay remains relatively unknown to visitors, giving it a peaceful, undiscovered quality. The bay faces north, making it warm and sheltered, with gentle conditions ideal for swimming and snorkelling. The drive from Picton passes through beautiful rural Sounds country with views over Port Underwood, historically significant as a base for early shore-based whaling operations. There are basic facilities at the beach reserve but no shops, so bring everything you need. The bay is also accessible as part of longer cycling and walking routes through the area. A true hidden gem for those willing to explore beyond the main tourist routes.

reviews.summary: A lovely hidden beach with beautiful clear water. The drive through the Sounds to get there is gorgeous. Bring a picnic and enjoy the peace — this is the Marlborough Sounds at its finest.

pois.name: Cloudy Bay Vineyard
pois.short: Iconic Marlborough winery that helped put New Zealand Sauvignon Blanc on the world stage since 1985.
pois.commentary: Cloudy Bay is perhaps the single winery most responsible for establishing New Zealand's international wine reputation. Founded in 1985 by David Hohnen, the Cloudy Bay Sauvignon Blanc became a global phenomenon, introducing the world to the intense, vibrant flavours of Marlborough's cool-climate terroir. The cellar door experience is refined and informative, with tastings covering the full range including Sauvignon Blanc, Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, and the sparkling Pelorus. The contemporary tasting room and garden area provide a stylish setting among the vines. Seasonal events include the popular Cloudy Bay Summer Sessions. Now owned by LVMH, the winery maintains its commitment to quality winemaking. Located on Jacksons Road in the heart of the Wairau Valley, it is easily combined with visits to neighbouring wineries along the Marlborough wine trail.

reviews.summary: A pilgrimage for Sauvignon Blanc lovers, with polished tastings in an elegant setting. The wines live up to the reputation. The cellar door experience is among Marlborough's best.

pois.name: Brancott Estate Heritage Centre
pois.short: Heritage centre at the site of Marlborough's first commercial vineyard, planted by Montana Wines in 1973.
pois.commentary: Brancott Estate stands on historically significant ground in New Zealand wine. It was here in 1973 that Montana Wines (now Brancott Estate) planted Marlborough's first commercial vineyard, an act that would transform the region and the entire New Zealand wine industry. The Heritage Centre, perched on a hillside with sweeping views across the Wairau Valley, tells this story through interactive displays and tasting experiences. The restaurant offers a seasonal menu designed to showcase wine and food pairing, with outdoor terrace seating overlooking the original vineyard blocks. The wine range covers Marlborough's signature Sauvignon Blanc alongside Pinot Noir and aromatic varieties. The location on Brancott Road provides views on clear days to the Richmond Ranges. A cornerstone visit for understanding Marlborough's wine story.

reviews.summary: The hilltop setting offers the best panoramic views of any Marlborough cellar door. The heritage story is fascinating and well-presented. The restaurant serves excellent food with well-matched wines.

pois.name: Allan Scott Family Winemakers
pois.short: Pioneer Marlborough family winery with a welcoming cellar door, garden bar, and craft brewery on site.
pois.commentary: Allan Scott was one of Marlborough's pioneering viticulturists, helping plant Montana's first vines in 1973 before establishing his own family winery in 1990. Now in its second generation, the winery produces a range of wines that showcase Marlborough's terroir, from zesty Sauvignon Blanc to elegant Pinot Noir and the popular Cecilia late-harvest Riesling. The cellar door on Jacksons Road is one of the region's most welcoming, with a relaxed garden bar and lawn area ideal for lingering over a glass. An on-site craft brewery, Moa Brewing, adds a beer dimension. The winery's twelve restaurant serves wood-fired pizzas and seasonal dishes. Located centrally on the Wairau Valley wine trail, it combines well with visits to nearby wineries. The family atmosphere and honest, well-priced wines make it a local favourite.

reviews.summary: A friendly, unpretentious cellar door with consistently good wines at fair prices. The garden setting is lovely for a relaxed afternoon. The addition of Moa beers gives non-wine-drinkers an option too.

pois.name: Framingham Wines
pois.short: Marlborough winery renowned for exceptional aromatic wines, particularly Riesling in multiple styles.
pois.commentary: Framingham has built an outstanding reputation for aromatic wines, particularly Riesling, which they produce in a range of styles from bone-dry to lusciously sweet. Founded in 1981 on Godfrey Road in the Wairau Valley, the winery's old vines and careful viticulture produce wines of remarkable concentration and complexity. Their Classic Riesling is considered one of New Zealand's benchmark examples of the variety, while the dessert-style Selection Riesling is liquid gold. The cellar door is a relaxed affair focused on letting the wines speak for themselves. Winemaker Andrew Hedley brings precision and passion to every vintage. Beyond Riesling, the Sauvignon Blanc, Pinot Gris, and Pinot Noir are all excellent. For wine enthusiasts who want to explore beyond Sauvignon Blanc, Framingham offers some of Marlborough's most rewarding tastings.

reviews.summary: A revelation for Riesling lovers, with stunning aromatic wines. The tasting experience is unpretentious and genuine. Often cited as a hidden favourite among Marlborough wine insiders.

pois.name: Queen Charlotte Sound Cruises
pois.short: Scenic cruises through the sheltered waterways of Queen Charlotte Sound from Picton harbour.
pois.commentary: Queen Charlotte Sound is a drowned river valley of extraordinary beauty, its sheltered waterways winding between bush-clad hills for over 40 kilometres from Picton to the open Cook Strait. Scenic cruises depart regularly from Picton, navigating through the sound's channels past secluded bays, island bird sanctuaries, historic homesteads, and marine farms where you can learn about mussel and salmon cultivation. Wildlife encounters include dolphins, seals, and numerous seabird species. Many cruises include stops at Ship Cove, where Captain Cook anchored five times during his Pacific explorations, and Motuara Island bird sanctuary. Mail boat cruises that deliver post to isolated communities along the sound offer a uniquely local experience. The calm, sheltered waters make cruising comfortable year-round. Kayaking and paddleboarding alternatives provide more intimate explorations of the bays. The sounds are also the gateway to the Queen Charlotte Track, a multi-day walking and cycling trail.

reviews.summary: A serene and scenic cruise through stunning waterways. The mail boat experience is uniquely authentic. Wildlife encounters are frequent and the bush-clad hills are beautiful in all seasons.

pois.name: Marlborough Wine Trail by Bike
pois.short: Flat cycling trail connecting over 30 Marlborough wineries on quiet rural roads through vineyard landscapes.
pois.commentary: The Marlborough wine region is ideally suited to cycling, with flat terrain, quiet rural roads, and over 30 cellar doors within easy riding distance. Multiple bike hire companies in Blenheim and Renwick offer standard and e-bikes with maps and suggested itineraries. The most popular route follows Rapaura Road through the heart of the Wairau Valley, connecting major wineries including Cloudy Bay, Allan Scott, Forrest, and Framingham. Self-guided tours allow riders to choose their own pace and stops, while guided group tours provide structure and local knowledge. E-bikes have made the trail accessible to all fitness levels, and the flat terrain means even non-cyclists can enjoy a wine-by-bike experience. Most cellar doors welcome cyclists, and several wineries have restaurants for lunch. The grape harvest season (March-April) is particularly atmospheric. Cycling between wineries is not only practical but adds to the enjoyment, keeping the tasting experience responsible and the scenery immersive.

reviews.summary: The perfect way to experience Marlborough's wineries. The flat terrain and quiet roads make cycling enjoyable even for non-cyclists. E-bikes are a game-changer for covering more ground between cellar doors.

pois.name: Kaikoura Whale Watch
pois.short: Year-round whale watching off Kaikoura where deep ocean currents bring sperm whales close to shore.
pois.commentary: Kaikoura Whale Watch offers one of the world's most reliable whale watching experiences, with giant sperm whales resident year-round in the deep Kaikoura Canyon just kilometres offshore. The unique underwater topography, where the continental shelf drops abruptly to over 1,000 metres, creates upwellings of nutrient-rich water that attract marine life from the smallest plankton to the largest whales. Boat tours take around 2.5 hours, with hydrophone technology used to locate whales before approaching for surface viewing. Sperm whales, the main attraction, can reach 18 metres in length and are often seen resting at the surface before their dramatic diving displays. Seasonal visitors include humpback whales, orca, blue whales, and southern right whales. Dusky dolphins, fur seals, and albatross are frequently seen. The Maori-owned operation is a model of indigenous tourism and conservation. Kaikoura is about 2.5 hours north of Christchurch on SH1. Booking well ahead is essential.

reviews.summary: An extraordinary marine wildlife experience with near-guaranteed whale sightings. The sperm whales are awe-inspiring in scale. A world-class ecotourism operation that supports local Maori community.

pois.name: Hans Herzog Estate & Restaurant
pois.short: European-influenced winery and fine dining in the heart of Marlborough wine country.
pois.commentary: Hans Herzog Estate brings a distinctly European sensibility to Marlborough wine country, founded by Swiss-born winemaker Hans Herzog who planted grapes that many said wouldn't grow in New Zealand, including Montepulciano, Viognier, and Nebbiolo. The results have silenced doubters, with the estate producing some of the most distinctive wines in the region. The on-site restaurant is widely regarded as one of the finest in the South Island, serving cuisine that draws on European traditions with New Zealand ingredients. The intimate dining room overlooks the vineyard. The estate also has luxury accommodation for guests wanting to immerse themselves in the wine country experience. Booking essential for dining. Located on Jeffries Road in the heart of the Wairau Valley wine district.

reviews.summary: Exceptional wine and dining that stands apart in Marlborough. The European varieties are fascinating. One of the South Island's best restaurant experiences.

pois.name: Omaka Aviation Heritage Centre — Knights of the Sky
pois.short: World-renowned WWI and WWII aviation exhibits featuring Peter Jackson's rare aircraft collection and cinematic dioramas.
pois.commentary: Omaka Aviation Heritage Centre houses two extraordinary exhibitions that blur the line between museum and film set. Knights of the Sky covers WWI aviation with original and replica aircraft displayed in dramatic dioramas created by Weta Workshop and featuring mannequins so lifelike they startle visitors. The Dangerous Skies exhibition covers WWII with the same cinematic treatment. Many of the aircraft belong to Sir Peter Jackson's personal collection — including extremely rare types that exist nowhere else. The attention to detail is staggering, from mud-spattered fuselages to theatrical lighting that evokes the drama of aerial combat. Located at Omaka Aerodrome near Blenheim, the centre also hosts a biennial Classic Fighters airshow. One of the finest aviation museums in the world, and a must-visit even for those with no particular interest in planes.

reviews.summary: A world-class aviation museum that astonishes even non-aviation enthusiasts. The Weta Workshop dioramas are breathtakingly realistic. Peter Jackson's aircraft collection is unrivalled.

pois.name: Edwin Fox Maritime Museum, Picton
pois.short: The world's ninth-oldest surviving ship — built in 1853, she carried troops, convicts, and immigrants across the globe before retiring to Picton.
pois.commentary: The Edwin Fox is a teak sailing ship built in Sulhampstead, India in 1853 and now preserved in a purpose-built dry dock on Picton's waterfront. In her working life she carried troops to the Crimean War, transported convicts to Western Australia, and brought immigrants to New Zealand on gruelling four-month voyages. She ended her career as a storage hulk and freezer ship in Picton Harbour. Today, visitors can walk around and partially through the hull, which retains its original teak framing and copper sheathing. The adjacent museum tells the ship's story through interactive displays, passenger diaries, and artefacts recovered during conservation. She is one of only a handful of ships from the age of sail still in existence anywhere in the world, and her survival in Picton is remarkable.

reviews.summary: A fascinating piece of living maritime history right on Picton's waterfront. The ship's varied career reads like a novel. The museum does an excellent job of bringing the stories of crew and passengers to life.

pois.name: Picton Heritage & Whaling Museum
pois.short: Extensive collections exploring the past of Picton and the Marlborough Sounds — whaling, settlement, and maritime history.
pois.commentary: The Picton Heritage and Whaling Museum occupies a charming old building near the waterfront and houses a dense collection of artefacts from the town's colourful past. Whaling was the economic engine of the Sounds for decades, and the museum displays harpoons, try-pots, photographs, and personal effects of the whalers who worked these waters. Maritime exhibits cover the history of the interisland ferry service, coastal trading vessels, and the many shipwrecks of the Sounds. Settlement-era photographs show Picton growing from a rough harbour camp to a proper town. The museum is volunteer-run and its keepers are walking encyclopedias of local history. A small entry fee supports ongoing preservation.

reviews.summary: A charming volunteer-run museum packed with local history. The whaling displays are confronting and fascinating in equal measure. The volunteers bring the collections alive with their knowledge.

pois.name: Pelorus Bridge Scenic Reserve
pois.short: Crystal-clear river pools for swimming surrounded by tall beech and podocarp forest — a classic New Zealand swimming hole.
pois.commentary: Pelorus Bridge Scenic Reserve sits where State Highway 6 crosses the Pelorus River between Havelock and Nelson, and it is one of the South Island's most inviting swimming spots. The river runs gin-clear over smooth boulders, forming deep pools that glow emerald green in the sunlight. Tall beech and podocarp forest shades the banks, and short bush walks loop through the reserve, including a 30-minute circular track through towering kahikatea and rimu. A longer walk follows the river upstream. The reserve has a well-maintained DOC campsite and picnic area. The swimming scene from The Hobbit: The Desolation of Smaug — the barrel-riding sequence — was filmed in the Pelorus River nearby. On a warm day, there are few better places in Marlborough to cool off.

reviews.summary: One of the South Island's best swimming spots with stunning native bush. The river pools are crystal clear and the forest is magnificent. The Hobbit connection adds a fun footnote.

pois.name: Queen Charlotte Sound Cruise & Walk
pois.short: Combine boat and walking in the beautiful Marlborough Sounds — water taxis drop you at secluded bays to walk between.
pois.commentary: Queen Charlotte Sound is a drowned river valley of extraordinary beauty, with forested ridges dropping steeply to sheltered bays and hidden coves. The best way to experience it is to combine water taxi and walking — operators from Picton will drop you at one bay and collect you from another, letting you walk a section of the Queen Charlotte Track with just a daypack. Popular day-walk sections include Ship Cove to Furneaux Lodge (15 km, 4-5 hours) or Furneaux Lodge to Punga Cove (8 km, 2.5 hours). The boats themselves offer superb scenic cruising through the sounds, often spotting dolphins, king shag, and blue penguins. Mail boat cruises that deliver to remote properties offer a full-day immersion. The flexibility of the water-taxi system means you can tailor a day to your fitness level and interests.

reviews.summary: A superb way to experience the Sounds without committing to the full multi-day track. The water-taxi system is brilliantly flexible. Dolphins and pristine bush bays are regular highlights.

pois.name: Te Kahu o Waipuna / Marlborough Art Gallery
pois.short: Marlborough's combined gallery and library — rotating exhibitions of regional and national art in a modern, light-filled space.
pois.commentary: Te Kahu o Waipuna is Blenheim's combined library and art gallery, housed in a modern building in the town centre. The gallery spaces host a rotating programme of exhibitions featuring Marlborough artists alongside touring national shows. The focus leans toward contemporary New Zealand art, with regular exhibitions exploring landscape, identity, and the relationship between people and place. The building itself is well-designed, with generous natural light and a pleasant cafe. Entry to the gallery is free. It makes a good rainy-day option in a region better known for its outdoor attractions, and the quality of exhibitions regularly surprises visitors who weren't expecting much from a small-town gallery.

reviews.summary: A pleasant surprise in Blenheim — the exhibitions are better than you might expect. The modern building is light and welcoming. Free entry makes it an easy stop between winery visits.

pois.name: Snout Track / Robin Hood Bay
pois.short: A beautiful coastal walk through native bush with panoramic views over the Marlborough Sounds and Cook Strait.
pois.commentary: The Snout Track begins near Whites Bay, south of Picton, and follows a ridgeline through regenerating native bush to Robin Hood Bay, offering panoramic views over the outer Marlborough Sounds and across Cook Strait to the North Island on clear days. The walk is moderate in difficulty, with some steady climbing on well-formed tracks. The route passes through a mix of kanuka, broadleaf forest, and coastal scrub, with birdlife including tui, bellbird, and kereru. Robin Hood Bay itself is a secluded beach at the end of a gravel road — a peaceful spot for a swim or picnic. The walk can be done as a return trip or as a one-way with a car shuttle. Allow about three hours for the full walk. It is far less crowded than the Queen Charlotte Track and rewards with equally impressive views.

reviews.summary: A rewarding coastal walk with views that rival the Queen Charlotte Track but without the crowds. Robin Hood Bay is a hidden gem. A well-kept local secret.

pois.name: Flaxbourne / Ward Beach Seal Colony
pois.short: A large New Zealand fur seal colony on a remote and dramatic stretch of coast between Blenheim and Kaikoura.
pois.commentary: The Flaxbourne coast near Ward is one of Marlborough's wildest and least-visited stretches of coastline, where rocky platforms and boulder beaches are home to a large colony of New Zealand fur seals. Dozens of seals bask on the rocks, pups play in tidal pools, and bulls guard their territories with impressive vocal displays. The coastline itself is dramatic — uplifted limestone platforms, sea stacks, and wave-cut terraces shaped by the same tectonic forces that built the Kaikoura Ranges nearby. Access is via a short walk from the Ward Beach car park off State Highway 1. There are no facilities, so bring water and sun protection. The site is less well-known than the Kaikoura seal colonies, which means you may have the seals to yourself. Keep a safe distance — fur seals can move fast and bite.

reviews.summary: A wild and uncrowded alternative to the Kaikoura seal colonies. The dramatic coastal scenery adds to the experience. Keep your distance from the seals and enjoy the solitude.

pois.name: The Marlborough Flyer Heritage Train
pois.short: A heritage steam train running along the scenic Picton-Blenheim line — vintage carriages and coastal views.
pois.commentary: The Marlborough Flyer is a heritage steam train that runs between Picton and Blenheim on the scenic coastal railway line. Vintage carriages restored to their former glory carry passengers along a route that hugs the coastline of the Wairau Plain, passing through farmland, vineyards, and alongside the sparkling waters of the sounds. The locomotive is a restored New Zealand Railways steam engine, and the rhythmic chug and whistle evoke an era when rail was king. Commentary on board covers the history of the railway and the region. The journey takes about an hour each way, with time to explore at each end. It runs on selected days — check the schedule and book ahead as carriages have limited capacity. A wonderful way to experience the landscape at a gentler pace.

reviews.summary: A delightful heritage experience with genuine vintage charm. The coastal scenery from the train is lovely. Check the schedule as it runs on limited days.

pois.name: Havelock Museum
pois.short: A small museum telling the story of Havelock — from gold rush town to the green-lipped mussel capital of New Zealand.
pois.commentary: Havelock Museum is a small, volunteer-run heritage museum in the centre of this quiet Sounds township that was once a booming gold town. Displays trace the town's transformation from the Wakamarina gold rush of the 1860s, through the timber-milling era, to its current identity as the green-lipped mussel capital of New Zealand. Photographs, mining equipment, and personal effects tell the stories of the people who built the town. The museum also covers the Pelorus Sound boat-building tradition and the mail boat runs that still serve remote properties in the sounds. Ernest Rutherford, the Nobel Prize-winning physicist who split the atom, attended Havelock School as a boy — the museum marks this connection with pride. Entry is by donation and the volunteers are enthusiastic storytellers.

reviews.summary: A charming small-town museum with stories that punch above its weight. The Rutherford connection is a nice surprise. The volunteers bring the gold rush era to life.

pois.name: Ship Cove / Meretoto Walk
pois.short: Walk from Captain Cook's favourite New Zealand anchorage through native bush along the Queen Charlotte Track.
pois.commentary: Ship Cove (Meretoto) is where Captain James Cook anchored five times between 1770 and 1777, using the sheltered bay as a base for exploring, resupplying, and charting the New Zealand coast. A monument marks the spot where Cook came ashore, and the cove remains largely unchanged — a crescent of bush-fringed beach in a deep, quiet inlet. The walk from Ship Cove to Furneaux Lodge (15 km) is the most popular day section of the Queen Charlotte Track, following a well-graded path through mature podocarp-broadleaf forest along the ridgeline above Queen Charlotte Sound. Views open up across the sound at several points. Water taxis from Picton drop walkers at Ship Cove and collect them from Furneaux Lodge, where cold drinks and food await. The combination of history, native bush, and sound views makes this one of the finest day walks in the top of the South Island.

reviews.summary: A walk steeped in history through magnificent native bush. The water-taxi logistics make it effortless. Standing where Cook stood gives a powerful sense of connection to New Zealand's story.

pois.name: Spy Valley & Wairau Valley Wineries
pois.short: Cycle between cellar doors in the famous Wairau Valley — home of the world's finest Sauvignon Blanc.
pois.commentary: The Wairau Valley is the engine room of Marlborough wine and the birthplace of the New Zealand Sauvignon Blanc style that took the world by storm. Spy Valley Wines sits at the western end of the valley, producing benchmark Sauvignon Blanc, Pinot Noir, and Riesling from sustainably farmed vineyards. But the real joy is the cycling — flat, quiet roads connect dozens of cellar doors within easy pedalling distance, making this one of the best wine-by-bike experiences anywhere. Hire bikes from Blenheim or join a guided cycle tour that handles the logistics. Between tastings, the valley floor stretches to the Richmond Ranges, and the light has a luminous quality that helps explain why grapes thrive here. Most cellar doors are open daily without appointment. Combine with lunch at one of the vineyard restaurants for a full day of indulgence.

reviews.summary: The definitive Marlborough wine experience — cycling between cellar doors is pure pleasure. The Sauvignon Blanc is world-class. Flat terrain and quiet roads make it accessible for all fitness levels.

pois.name: EcoTours Dolphin Swimming, Picton
pois.short: Swim with dolphins in the sheltered waters of the Marlborough Sounds.
pois.commentary: Bottlenose and dusky dolphins are resident year-round. The company also spots NZ fur seals and king shags.

reviews.summary: Swimming with dolphins in the Sounds is exhilarating.

pois.name: Pelorus Mail Boat
pois.short: Join the NZ Post rural delivery boat as it delivers mail to remote communities in Pelorus Sound.
pois.commentary: The Pelorus Mail Boat has been delivering mail since 1918. A full-day trip providing authentic glimpses of remote Sound life.

reviews.summary: One of the most authentic tourism experiences in NZ.

pois.name: Marlborough Wine Trail
pois.short: NZ’s largest wine region — over 150 wineries producing the Sauvignon Blanc that put NZ on the world wine map.
pois.commentary: Marlborough produces over 75% of NZ’s wine. Cycling between cellar doors is ideal. Key cellar doors include Cloudy Bay, Villa Maria, Fromm.

reviews.summary: Wine lovers rate it as a world-class wine trail.

pois.name: Ohau Point Seal Colony
pois.short: A large NZ fur seal colony right beside the road — including a secret waterfall pool where seal pups play.
pois.commentary: Dozens of seals bask beside the road. The hidden gem is a 10-minute walk up the Ohau Stream to a waterfall where seal pups gather to play (June–October). Free access.

reviews.summary: The seal pup waterfall is absolutely magical — one of NZ’s best free wildlife experiences.

pois.name: Marlborough Museum, Blenheim
pois.short: Marlborough's regional museum in Brayshaw Heritage Park — with collections spanning the Wairau Bar's earliest settlers, the wine industry, and the region's colonial heritage.
pois.commentary: The Marlborough Museum at 26 Arthur Baker Place in Brayshaw Heritage Park houses a broad collection interpreting the region's full history: from Rangitāne and Ngāti Toa occupation, through the 1843 Wairau Affray and European settlement, to the rise of the modern wine industry. Standout exhibits include archaeological material from the Wairau Bar (the earliest human settlement in New Zealand), the wine industry's transformation of the region, and extensive photographic and textile collections. The Blenheim Riverside Railway — a miniature heritage railway running from Riverside Park to Brayshaw — makes this accessible and fun for families. The museum archives are open by appointment for genealogical research.

reviews.summary: The Marlborough Museum is praised for its honest interpretation of the region's full history, including its pre-European and contact-era layers. The Wairau Bar material is described as exceptional for understanding how far back human settlement in this landscape reaches. Good value for time spent.

pois.name: Marlborough Art Gallery Te Kahu o Waipuna
pois.short: Marlborough's public art gallery in central Blenheim — free entry, with changing exhibitions connecting regional and national contemporary art.
pois.commentary: The Marlborough Art Gallery at 15 High Street, Blenheim, opened as the Millennium Public Art Gallery in December 1999 in a renovated building opposite Seymour Square. One of New Zealand's youngest public galleries, it has developed a reputation for well-curated exhibitions that draw on the national art community while maintaining strong connections to regional artists. Admission is free, and the gallery runs approximately 12 exhibitions per year. Its Te Kahu o Waipuna name — 'the shelter of the sacred pool' — connects it to the Wairau River and the landscape within which Marlborough's identity is rooted. The gallery is open 7 days and functions as a cultural anchor in central Blenheim.

reviews.summary: The Marlborough Art Gallery is praised for the quality of its changing exhibitions and its free admission policy. Visitors appreciate having a serious contemporary gallery in a wine-region town, and the free entry makes it a low-risk cultural stop between winery visits.

pois.name: Tuamarina Cemetery & Wairau Affray Memorial
pois.short: Marlborough's most historic cemetery on Cotterill Street, Tuamarina — with the 1869 memorial to the 1843 Wairau Affray, the first armed clash between Ngāti Toa and European settlers.
pois.commentary: Tuamarina Cemetery on the hilltop overlooking Tuamarina village contains the 1869 monument erected to mark the site of the Wairau Affray of 17 June 1843. The affray occurred when an armed party of Nelson settlers confronted Ngāti Toa rangatira Te Rauparaha and Te Rangihaeata over the disputed survey of Wairau land. The confrontation resulted in the deaths of 22 Europeans and 4–6 Māori. Governor FitzRoy subsequently ruled that the settlers were in the wrong — an unusual early acknowledgement of Māori rights. The cemetery is Marlborough's oldest, and the memorial occupies a hilltop position that looks across the flats toward the Wairau River where the events unfolded. A pull-off site on State Highway 1 immediately north of Tuamarina marks the location of the first shots.

reviews.summary: Tuamarina Cemetery is described as a place where the weight of 19th-century New Zealand history becomes tangible and real. Visitors who take the time to read the memorial and the surrounding context describe it as one of the most honest heritage sites in the country. A short but significant stop on the Blenheim-Nelson route.

pois.name: Te Rae o Karaka / Karaka Point
pois.short: A historic reserve on a narrow headland near Picton — with a carved pou erected by Rangitāne depicting Kupe's defeat of the giant octopus Te Wheke-o-Muturangi.
pois.commentary: Te Rae o Karaka Historic Reserve occupies a narrow headland projecting into Totaranui (Queen Charlotte Sound), 8–9 km northeast of Picton between Waikawa and Whatamango Bays. The site was a pā of Ngāti Toa, attacked in the early 1830s during Te Rauparaha's campaigns through the region. Rangitāne, the tangata whenua of Marlborough, have erected a carved pou (marker post) on the headland depicting the foundational story of Kupe — the great Polynesian navigator — and his defeat of Te Wheke-o-Muturangi, the giant octopus, in these very waters. The pou connects this specific headland to the deep mythological geography of the Marlborough Sounds. The reserve is accessible by road and offers views across the Sound.
sources_to_check: Marlborough NZ Te Rae o Karaka

reviews.summary: Karaka Point is praised as one of the most layered cultural sites in the Picton area — the carved pou, the pā history, and the connection to the Kupe mythology make it far more than a scenic viewpoint. Visitors who research the story in advance describe it as a profoundly rewarding stop.

pois.name: Rangitāne o Wairau Cultural Heritage Trail
pois.short: A self-guided cultural trail connecting the key sites of Rangitāne o Wairau heritage across the Marlborough region — from Wairau Bar to the Sounds.
pois.commentary: Rangitāne o Wairau are the tangata whenua of the Marlborough region, with genealogical connections to the Kurahaupo waka and occupation of the Wairau and Sounds for centuries before European contact. The cultural heritage trail connects Te Pokohiwi (Wairau Bar), the Tuamarina Wairau Affray site, the Rangitāne House in Blenheim, Parerarua Marae in the Wairau Valley, and the Karaka Point pou near Picton. Interpretive panels and the Marlborough Museum's Wairau Bar exhibition provide the context for a journey through one of New Zealand's most archaeologically significant landscapes. Rangitāne's official tourism and cultural programme — in development — aims to offer guided experiences directly with iwi members.

reviews.summary: The Rangitāne cultural heritage trail is praised by visitors who self-guide it as a transformative way to understand Marlborough — connecting the wine and scenery landscape to a human story of extraordinary depth and complexity. The Wairau Bar is described as the emotional anchor of the experience.

pois.name: Pelorus Bridge Scenic Reserve / Te Hoiere
pois.short: One of Marlborough's last stands of river-flat podocarp forest — a DOC reserve on Te Hoiere (Pelorus River) with swimming holes, forest walks, and the filming location for The Hobbit barrel-riding scene.
pois.commentary: Pelorus Bridge Scenic Reserve protects a remnant of the lowland podocarp-broadleaf forest that once lined the Pelorus/Te Hoiere river plain — rimu, kahikatea, and tōtara trees that survived when most of the valley was cleared for farming. The reserve's river pools are crystal-clear and deep, making them among the most popular swimming spots in Marlborough. Peter Jackson filmed the barrel-riding escape scene from The Hobbit: The Desolation of Smaug here in 2012, adding a contemporary dimension to the reserve's appeal. The trails include the Totara and Rimu walks through the old forest, and the DOC campsite is one of the region's finest. Originally known as Te Hoiere, the river was renamed Pelorus by early European explorers for the navigational bearing instrument.

reviews.summary: Pelorus Bridge earns strong reviews as one of the most beautiful DOC reserves in Marlborough — the old forest, the clear river, and the swimming holes combine to make it a place visitors return to year after year. The Hobbit connection draws younger visitors who then discover the forest is the real highlight.

pois.name: Havelock — Greenshell Mussel Capital
pois.short: The self-proclaimed Greenshell Mussel Capital of the World — a working fishing village at the head of Pelorus Sound where Marlborough's mussel farms produce 60% of New Zealand's crop.
pois.commentary: Havelock sits at the confluence of the Pelorus and Kaituna rivers, 35 km west of Blenheim on SH6, and is the operational centre of Marlborough's mussel aquaculture industry. The sheltered Pelorus Sound behind the town hosts hundreds of mussel farms on long-lines — Greenshell mussels (Perna canaliculus) that grow fat in the plankton-rich waters. Marlborough produces over 80% of New Zealand's farmed mussels by export value. The Mussel Pot restaurant on the main street serves the full range — fresh, steamed, baked, in chowder — at working-town prices. The town is also the departure point for the Pelorus Mail Boat Cruise. Havelock is the birthplace of two Nobel laureates: Ernest Rutherford (physics, 1908) and Maurice Wilkins (medicine, 1962).

reviews.summary: Havelock delivers exactly what it promises — excellent, fresh, affordable mussels in a working waterfront setting that has not been prettified for tourists. Visitors describe The Mussel Pot as a genuine food experience. The dual Nobel laureate connection surprises most visitors and adds an unexpected intellectual dimension to the stop.

pois.name: Te Rauparaha & Ngāti Toa in the Sounds
pois.short: The story of Ngāti Toa's arrival in the Marlborough Sounds in the 1820s under Te Rauparaha — one of history's most remarkable strategic migrations, reshaping the top of the South Island.
pois.commentary: In the 1820s, under the leadership of Te Rauparaha — possibly the most militarily significant rangatira in New Zealand history — Ngāti Toa migrated from the Kāwhia Harbour on the Waikato coast to Kāpiti Island and the Marlborough Sounds, displacing or absorbing existing tribes. The migration was enabled by muskets acquired through trade with Europeans, and it fundamentally reshaped the demographic and political landscape of the top of the South Island. Te Rauparaha's base at Robin Hood Bay in Port Underwood became the operational centre for Ngāti Toa's control of the Cook Strait. The haka 'Ka Mate' — now known globally — was composed by Te Rauparaha at a Rotorua pā in this period. The Marlborough Sounds became Ngāti Toa's rohe, and their presence shaped the 1840 Treaty signings and the subsequent land transactions that led to the Wairau Affray.

reviews.summary: This interpretive site consistently earns praise for the quality of historical storytelling — Te Rauparaha's story is described by visitors as one of the most extraordinary in New Zealand history, and understanding it transforms how the Sounds landscape reads. Best experienced with a cultural guide who can bring the oral tradition to the physical landscape.

pois.name: Robin Hood Bay / Te Rauparaha's Stronghold
pois.short: A remote Port Underwood bay where Te Rauparaha maintained a home in the 1830s — and one of the earliest European shore-whaling stations in the Marlborough Sounds.
pois.commentary: Robin Hood Bay in Port Underwood was simultaneously a Ngāti Toa stronghold and an early European shore-whaling station in the 1820s and 1830s — a collision of cultures that was both commercially productive and profoundly disruptive. Te Rauparaha had a house in the bay, and it was from this base that Ngāti Toa maintained their dominance over the Cook Strait crossing. Shore whalers from Sydney and London established stations here to process southern right whales, and the interaction between their crews and Ngāti Toa was a template for the complicated first contact relationships of early New Zealand. The bay is accessed via the Port Underwood Road — a winding, partly gravel road that is itself a heritage experience — and rewards the effort with dramatic coastal scenery and a sense of standing at the edge of New Zealand's colonial frontier.

reviews.summary: Robin Hood Bay is described by visitors who make the effort as one of the most historically resonant and physically beautiful places in Marlborough — the combination of the drive, the landscape, and the layered history makes it feel genuinely exploratory. Not for those in a hurry.

pois.name: Pelorus Bridge Scenic Reserve
pois.short: Crystal-clear swimming holes in native bush, used as a Hobbit filming location.
pois.commentary: Pelorus Bridge Scenic Reserve protects a remnant of lowland podocarp forest along the Pelorus River, whose jade-green swimming holes gained fame as a filming location for The Hobbit. The short bush walks wind through towering kahikatea, rimu, and matai trees. The DOC campsite is one of the best in the region. The reserve sits on SH6 between Blenheim and Nelson.

reviews.summary: Beautiful bush and gorgeous swimming. The camping is fantastic. Hobbit fans love the filming location connection.

pois.name: Allan Scott Family Winemakers
pois.short: Pioneer Marlborough winery with cellar door tastings and vineyard restaurant.
pois.commentary: Allan Scott was one of the original Marlborough wine pioneers, planting vines in 1973. Today the family winery produces a well-regarded range of Sauvignon Blanc, Pinot Noir, and Riesling. The cellar door offers relaxed tastings and the Twelve Trees restaurant serves seasonal Marlborough produce. The winery sits in the heart of the Wairau Valley wine district.

reviews.summary: Excellent wines with genuine pioneer history. The restaurant is one of the best in Marlborough's wine district.

pois.name: Whale Watch Kaikoura
pois.short: Year-round whale watching with giant sperm whales in their natural deep-water habitat.
pois.commentary: Whale Watch Kaikoura is New Zealand's premier marine wildlife experience, offering year-round encounters with giant sperm whales in the deep underwater canyon just kilometres offshore. Tours run 2-3 times daily on purpose-built catamarans equipped with hydrophones. The Maori-owned operation has transformed Kaikoura from a struggling fishing town into a thriving eco-tourism destination. Book well ahead in summer.

reviews.summary: An awe-inspiring encounter with the ocean's giants. The sperm whales are magnificent. Maori-owned and operated.

pois.name: Havelock / Green-Lipped Mussel Capital
pois.short: Self-proclaimed Green-Lipped Mussel Capital of the World with seafood restaurants.
pois.commentary: Havelock is a small fishing village at the head of Pelorus Sound that has earned the title Green-Lipped Mussel Capital of the World, with the surrounding Marlborough Sounds producing the majority of New Zealand's mussel harvest. The town's waterfront restaurants serve mussels every way imaginable — steamed, smoked, battered, and in chowder — with Mussel Pot and the Mussel Boys being the best-known options. The town is also the departure point for mail boat and water taxi services deep into Pelorus Sound, offering a working alternative to tourist cruises. The Pelorus Bridge scenic reserve, with its Hobbit filming location, is a 15-minute drive west. Havelock is the quieter, more authentic counterpart to Picton.

reviews.summary: Outstanding mussels in a charming fishing village. The Mussel Pot is famous for good reason. Mail boat trips into the Sounds are excellent.

pois.name: Wairau Valley Wine Trail / Blenheim
pois.short: Heart of NZ's largest wine region with 100+ wineries on flat cycling terrain.
pois.commentary: The Wairau Valley around Blenheim is the epicentre of New Zealand's wine revolution, home to over 100 wineries and 30 cellar doors within easy cycling distance. Marlborough Sauvignon Blanc put New Zealand wine on the world map, and tasting the flagship wine where it's grown is a pilgrimage for wine lovers. The flat terrain and quiet country roads make cycling between wineries the preferred exploration method. Key stops include Cloudy Bay, Brancott Estate (where Marlborough's first Sauvignon Blanc was planted in 1975), Framingham, and Nautilus. Many wineries offer food alongside tastings. The annual Marlborough Wine and Food Festival in February draws thousands. Bike hire and guided cycling wine tours are readily available from Blenheim and Renwick.

reviews.summary: World-class wines in a flat, cyclist-friendly landscape. The density of quality cellar doors is unmatched in NZ.

pois.name: Kaikoura Peninsula Seal Colony
pois.short: Large fur seal colony on coastal rocks with free walkway access.
pois.commentary: The Kaikoura Peninsula Walkway passes right alongside one of the most accessible New Zealand fur seal colonies in the country, where dozens of seals can be observed basking on rocks, playing in tidal pools, and surfing in the shorebreak — all from just a few metres away. The seals are accustomed to walkers and generally unconcerned by human presence, though DOC guidelines require a 10-metre distance. The walkway continues around the peninsula past limestone formations, Maori heritage sites, and excellent rock-pooling spots. The full peninsula loop takes about 3 hours. The seals are present year-round but are most numerous in winter when the colony swells. The walkway is free and starts from the town centre.

reviews.summary: Incredible close-up seal viewing — they're everywhere. The walkway is free and easy. Baby seals in winter are adorable.

pois.name: Blenheim Winery Circuit
pois.short: Cycle between Marlborough's world-famous Sauvignon Blanc producers.
pois.commentary: Marlborough is New Zealand's largest and most famous wine region, producing the crisp, aromatic Sauvignon Blanc that put New Zealand on the world wine map. The flat Wairau Valley around Blenheim is perfect for cycling between cellar doors — over 35 wineries are within easy cycling distance. Major names like Cloudy Bay, Brancott Estate, and Wither Hills sit alongside boutique producers making outstanding Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, and Riesling. Bike hire is widely available in Blenheim and Renwick. The combination of world-class wine, flat cycling terrain, and the beautiful valley setting makes this one of New Zealand's premier wine experiences.

reviews.summary: World-famous wines accessible by bike. The variety of cellar doors is excellent. Flat cycling makes it easy.

pois.name: Ship Cove / Meretoto — Captain Cook's Favourite Anchorage
pois.short: Captain Cook's favourite NZ anchorage, visited five times — start of the Queen Charlotte Track.
pois.commentary: Ship Cove (Meretoto) in the outer Queen Charlotte Sound was Captain Cook's favourite anchorage in New Zealand, visited on five separate occasions between 1770 and 1777. A memorial marks the site where Cook's crew set up an observatory, brewed spruce beer, and established vegetable gardens. The cove is only accessible by water taxi from Picton and serves as the northern starting point of the Queen Charlotte Track. The bush-clad cove is peaceful and atmospheric, and it's easy to understand why Cook returned repeatedly. The water taxi ride itself through the Sounds is scenic. Interpretive panels at the memorial explain Cook's visits and interactions with local Māori.

reviews.summary: Historically significant and beautifully peaceful. Only accessible by boat adds to the atmosphere. Good Queen Charlotte Track starting point.

pois.name: Havelock Mussel Cruise & Greenshell Tour
pois.short: Cruise the Pelorus Sound visiting mussel farms and tasting the freshest greenshell mussels imaginable.
pois.commentary: Havelock, self-proclaimed Greenshell Mussel Capital of the World, is the base for cruises into the Pelorus Sound that visit working mussel farms and provide the freshest mussel-tasting experience possible. Guides explain the farming process — from seeding to harvest — while the cruise navigates the beautiful, bush-clad waterways of the Sounds. The tasting of mussels steamed on board, minutes from harvest, is a revelation — plump, sweet, and utterly unlike anything from a supermarket. The Pelorus Sound scenery is stunning and less visited than the Queen Charlotte Sound. Havelock itself has good mussel restaurants for follow-up dining.

reviews.summary: The freshest mussels you will ever eat. The Sounds scenery is magnificent. Fascinating farming insight. Havelock is a great base.

pois.name: Kaikōura Peninsula Walkway
pois.short: Spectacular coastal walkway around the Kaikōura Peninsula with fur seals, seabirds, and mountain views.
pois.commentary: The Kaikōura Peninsula Walkway is a spectacular 3-hour coastal walk circling the peninsula where the Seaward Kaikōura Range meets the Pacific Ocean. The track passes a large fur seal colony at Point Kean, intertidal rock pools, seabird nesting areas, and limestone formations. Views alternate between the snow-capped mountains directly behind the coast and the open ocean where whales breach offshore. The walkway passes through areas of cultural significance to Ngāi Tahu, with interpretive panels explaining traditional food-gathering sites. The density of marine wildlife visible from the track is remarkable — fur seals, red-billed gulls, shags, and occasionally dolphins and whales visible from the clifftop.

reviews.summary: Outstanding coastal walk with incredible wildlife density. Mountains-meet-ocean scenery is dramatic. The fur seals are entertaining.

pois.name: Omaka Aviation Heritage Centre
pois.short: World-class aviation museum with WWI & WWII aircraft in dramatic dioramas by Weta Workshop.
pois.commentary: The Omaka Aviation Heritage Centre in Blenheim is one of the world's finest aviation museums, featuring rare WWI and WWII aircraft displayed in dramatic, life-size dioramas created by Peter Jackson's Weta Workshop. The Knights of the Sky exhibition covers WWI aviation with original and replica aircraft in cinematic battle scenes. The Dangerous Skies exhibition covers WWII with similarly dramatic displays. Peter Jackson's personal collection forms the core, and his passion for aviation history — combined with Weta's extraordinary model-making skills — creates displays of unmatched quality. Visiting aircraft enthusiasts consistently rate Omaka among the best aviation museums globally.

reviews.summary: World-class aviation displays by Weta Workshop. The WWI dioramas are jaw-dropping. A must-visit even for non-aviation enthusiasts.

pois.name: Picton Foreshore & Heritage Walk
pois.short: Waterfront walk in the gateway to the South Island — heritage buildings, the Edwin Fox, and harbour views.
pois.commentary: Picton's foreshore walk along the harbour combines maritime heritage, harbour views, and the chance to visit the Edwin Fox — the world's ninth-oldest ship, built in 1853. The walk follows the waterfront past the ferry terminal (gateway between the North and South Islands), fishing boats, and heritage buildings to the EcoWorld aquarium. The Edwin Fox Maritime Museum preserves the remarkable 170-year-old vessel, which served as a troopship, convict ship, and immigrant carrier. Picton's compact waterfront has good dining and café options. The town serves as the northern trailhead for the Queen Charlotte Track and base for Marlborough Sounds cruises. For most visitors, Picton is a brief transit point — but it rewards those who linger.

reviews.summary: Worth more than a quick ferry stop. The Edwin Fox is remarkable. Good cafes and harbour views. Gateway to the Sounds.

pois.name: Awatere Valley Wine Sub-Region
pois.short: Marlborough's emerging wine sub-region producing distinctive, mineral-driven Sauvignon Blanc.
pois.commentary: The Awatere Valley is Marlborough's emerging wine sub-region, south of the main Wairau Valley, producing Sauvignon Blanc with a distinctive mineral, herbaceous character that sets it apart from the broader Marlborough style. The valley is drier, cooler, and more wind-exposed than the Wairau, producing wines with greater intensity and complexity. Cellar doors are fewer and more intimate than in the Wairau — Yealands, Vavasour, and Delta are among the notable producers. The valley landscape is dramatic, with the Awatere River cutting through dry, golden hills. For wine enthusiasts wanting to go beyond mainstream Marlborough, the Awatere offers a more adventurous tasting experience.

reviews.summary: Distinctive wines from a dramatic landscape. More intimate than the main Wairau Valley. Wine enthusiasts should explore here.

pois.name: Pelorus Sound Mail Boat Cruise
pois.short: Full-day cruise delivering mail to remote Pelorus Sound homesteads.
pois.commentary: The Pelorus Sound Mail Boat run is one of New Zealand's most unique journey experiences — a full-day cruise aboard a working mail boat that delivers post, packages, and supplies to remote homesteads scattered throughout the intricate waterways of Pelorus Sound. Departing from Havelock (the green-mussel capital of the world), the boat navigates through narrow channels, past mussel farms, and into sheltered coves, visiting properties only accessible by water. The skipper shares stories of the Sound's history and residents while passengers enjoy morning and afternoon tea and a fresh mussel lunch. The route varies daily depending on delivery schedules. It's an authentic glimpse into remote rural New Zealand life. Book well ahead as the boat carries limited passengers.

reviews.summary: Passengers describe it as a unique, unforgettable experience. The scenery is stunning and the stories are fascinating. The mussel lunch is excellent. A Marlborough highlight.

pois.name: Ship Cove / Meretoto Heritage Walk
