pois.short: Walk in Captain Cook's footsteps at his favourite anchorage in the Marlborough Sounds — rich in maritime and natural history.
pois.commentary: Ship Cove (Meretoto) was Captain James Cook's favourite anchorage in New Zealand — he visited five times between 1770 and 1777, using the sheltered bay to rest and resupply his ships. Today the cove is the starting point for the Queen Charlotte Track, and a heritage walk along the shore traces the history of Cook's visits, the interactions with local Māori, and the astronomical observations he made here. Interpretive panels explain the significance of specific sites. Access is by water taxi from Picton. The bush and birdlife are superb, and the sense of historical connection is powerful.

reviews.summary: A moving historical site beautifully presented. The water taxi ride adds to the experience. The bush and birdlife are excellent. Good interpretation panels bring the history alive.

pois.name: Molesworth Station Heritage Drive
pois.short: New Zealand's largest cattle station, open for summer driving — a remote high-country adventure through tussock, rivers and 180,000 hectares of history.
pois.commentary: Molesworth Station covers 180,000 hectares of high country between Blenheim and Hanmer Springs — it's the largest farm in New Zealand. The road through the station is open to vehicles from late December to Easter (weather permitting) and offers one of the most remote driving experiences in the country. The route crosses the Awatere and Acheron Rivers, climbs through tussock basins rimmed by snow-capped ranges, and passes through country that has barely changed since the first runholders arrived in the 1850s. There are no facilities — bring fuel, food, water and a spare tyre. The solitude is magnificent.

reviews.summary: An extraordinary high-country driving experience. The remoteness is the main attraction. Only open in summer — check DOC website for dates. Come fully self-sufficient. Allow a full day.

pois.name: Tui Nature Reserve, Queen Charlotte Sound
pois.short: A predator-free forest sanctuary in the Marlborough Sounds where native birdsong has returned to extraordinary levels.
pois.commentary: Tui Nature Reserve occupies a peninsula in Queen Charlotte Sound that has been trapped and baited to near predator-free status, allowing native bird populations to recover dramatically. The result is a forest alive with birdsong — bellbird, tūī, kererū, fantail, weka, and robin all thrive here at densities rarely heard on the mainland. Walking tracks loop through the regenerating bush to golden sand beaches, and the water taxi from Picton drops you at the doorstep. It's a powerful example of what mainland predator control can achieve, and the soundtrack of the forest stays with you long after you leave.

reviews.summary: The birdsong is astonishing. The walks are well-maintained and scenic. Access by water taxi is easy. A great addition to a Sounds itinerary. Some tracks are steep.

pois.name: Pelorus River Kayaking (Hobbit Filming Location)
pois.short: Kayak down the crystal-clear Pelorus River — a stunning forest river made famous as a filming location for The Hobbit.
pois.commentary: The Pelorus River near Havelock achieved international fame as the filming location for the barrel-riding scene in The Hobbit: The Desolation of Smaug. The river is genuinely beautiful — crystal-clear water flowing through beech-forested gorges with deep swimming holes and rapids. Several operators offer guided kayak trips ranging from gentle half-day floats to full-day adventures including the Hobbit filming section. The swimming holes are superb on warm days — the water is cold but clean and invigorating. The surrounding Pelorus Bridge Scenic Reserve adds excellent walking options. Film fan or not, the river is stunning.

reviews.summary: A beautiful river kayaking experience. The water clarity is incredible. Hobbit fans love the film location. Guides are skilled. The swimming holes are refreshing. Best in summer when water levels are manageable.

pois.name: Queen Charlotte Track Day Walk
pois.short: Walk a section of one of New Zealand's finest coastal tracks — ridgeline views over the Marlborough Sounds on a day trip from Picton.
pois.commentary: The Queen Charlotte Track stretches 70 km through the Marlborough Sounds, but day walkers can enjoy its finest sections by combining water taxi transfers with walks between bays. The most popular day walk runs from Ship Cove to Furneaux Lodge (15 km, 4-5 hours), following a ridgeline with alternating views over Queen Charlotte and Kenepuru Sounds — deep blue water, bush-clad peninsulas, and distant mountain ranges. Water taxis from Picton handle the logistics, dropping you at the start and collecting from the finish. The track is well-maintained and graded moderate, suitable for anyone with reasonable fitness.

reviews.summary: One of New Zealand's most scenic day walks. The ridgeline views are spectacular. Water taxi logistics work seamlessly. Well-maintained track. Book water taxis ahead in summer.

pois.name: Edwin Fox Maritime Museum — Picton
pois.short: Museum housing the world's ninth-oldest surviving ship, built in 1853.
pois.commentary: The Edwin Fox is the world's ninth-oldest surviving ship, built in Calcutta in 1853 from teak. She carried troops in the Crimean War, transported convicts to Australia, and brought immigrants to New Zealand. The museum on Picton's waterfront tells her remarkable story.

reviews.summary: Fascinating old ship with an incredible history.

pois.name: Robin Hood Bay — Whites Bay Walk
pois.short: Coastal walk connecting two sheltered bays on the Marlborough coast.
pois.commentary: The track between Robin Hood Bay and Whites Bay follows the Marlborough coastline through regenerating bush with views across Cook Strait. Both bays offer sheltered swimming and are far quieter than the Sounds, with a pleasant bush walk connecting them.

reviews.summary: Lovely quiet coastal walk with two beautiful beaches.

pois.name: Ship Cove — Queen Charlotte Sound
pois.short: Captain Cook's favourite New Zealand anchorage at the head of Queen Charlotte Sound.
pois.commentary: Ship Cove is where Captain Cook spent the most time during his explorations of New Zealand, visiting five times between 1770 and 1777. Accessible by water taxi, the cove features a monument, interpretive panels, and the start of the Queen Charlotte Track — a place where Māori and European histories intersect.

reviews.summary: Deeply historic site in a stunning setting.

pois.name: Kenepuru Sound Kayaking
pois.short: Paddle the quieter inner waterways of the Marlborough Sounds.
pois.commentary: Kenepuru Sound is the quieter, more intimate alternative to Queen Charlotte Sound. Its sheltered waters wind between bush-clad ridges, with opportunities to spot dolphins, blue penguins, and king shags. Kayaking here offers a genuine sense of wilderness exploration.

reviews.summary: Wonderfully peaceful — the less-visited side of the Sounds.

pois.name: Blenheim Heritage Walk & Seymour Square
pois.short: Heritage town walk through Blenheim's Victorian gardens and historic precinct.
pois.commentary: Blenheim's heritage walk connects the Victorian-era Seymour Square gardens with the town's historic buildings, including the clock tower, former courthouse, and early settler landmarks. The gardens feature mature trees, a band rotunda, and war memorial — a pleasant stroll through Marlborough's history.

reviews.summary: Pleasant heritage walk — the gardens are well maintained.
